coredata.dc:title: Micro/nano bubble-modified flame atomic spectrometry as a new technique for promotion of the figures of merit during determination of metal species: Improvement in the performance of pre-mixed burner
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Doroodmand, Mohammad Mahdi
Mehrtash, Maryam
coredata.dc:description: A new micro/nano bubble-modified flame atomic spectrometry has been introduced for promotion of some figures of some merit during determination of metal elements such as Pb(II) and Sn(II) via controlling the stoichiometry of an acetylene/air flame by bubbles generated through sonoelectrochemistry on the surface of nanocarbons such as carbon dots (CDs). For this purpose, the sonoelectrochemical cell is connected to a pre-mixed burner to generate both oxygen and hydrogen micro/nano bubbles during applying direct-current (DC) potential such as +8.00V (for oxygen) or −8.00V(for hydrogen) to a two-electrode system including a graphite rod and stainless steel inside a solution of 0.5M NaCl as electrolyte. According to the results, introduction of each oxygen or hydrogen micro/nano bubbles to the flame atomizer not only has strong influence on controlling the stoichiometry (oxidizing and reducing behavior) and temperature of the flame, but also prevents from flashback of the flame into the pre-mixed burner. Major improvements is also observed in some figures of merit such as linearity, sensitivity, detection limit, and the selectivity during analyses of elements such as Pb(II) or Sn(II) via modification of atomic absorption/emission spectrometry (AAS, AES) with micro/nano bubbles. Generated bubbles also play significant roles like releasing and chelating agents during analysis of Pb(II). Good agreements have been evaluated between bubble-modified AAS and inductively coupled plasma during analyses of Pb(II) in some types of rice samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Micro/nano bubbles
Atomizer
Flashback
Nanocarbons
Atomic Spectrometry
Flame

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of thermally-stable elements by bubble-modified flame
Effect of micro/nano bubbles in atomic emission spectroscopy

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Instrumentation system of micro/nano bubbles-modified pre-mixed burner.
Effect of micro/nano bubbles on the flame temperature during formation of (A) O2, and (B) H2 micro/nano bubbles at different temperatures.
Effect of O2 micro/nano bubbles on (A) concentration of oxygen and (B) pressure gradient at different DC voltages applied to the electrode system.
Photographic images of the flame including (A, B) before and (C, D) after introduction of H2 and O2 micro/nano bubbles, respectively.
Calibration curves revealing compassion between (A) general AAS and (B) bubble-introduced AAS and during analysis of Pb(II).

Histograms showing the effect of O2 and H2 micro/nano bubbles on the elimination of (A) 50- and (B) 100-fold excess of foreign species such as F−, CO3 2−, PO4 3− during analysis of 10μgmL−1 of Pb(II).

Histograms showing the effect of ultrasound on the efficiency of O2 and H2 micro/nano bubbles on the elimination of (A) 50- and (B) 100-fold excess of F−, as interfering agent during analysis of 10.0μgmL−1 of Pb(II).

Calibration curves showing the effect of micro/nano bubbles as chelating agent during analysis of different concatenations of Pb(II) standard solutions, comparison between EDTA and H2/O2 micro/nano bubbles.
Calibration curves showing the effect of O2 and H2 micro/nano bubbles for analysis of different concatenations of Sn(II) standard solutions.
Calibration curves showing the effect of H2 and O2 micro/nano bubbles on the atomic emission of for analysis of different concatenations of Pb(II) standard solutions.
Comparison between AAS and bubble-based AAS method for Pb(II).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The data are the average of three replicate analyses.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison between AAS and bubble-based AAS method for Sn(II).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The data are the average of three replicate analyses.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The data are the average of three replicate analyses.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: ICP is considered as reference method.

head.ce:title: Micro/nano bubble-modified flame atomic spectrometry as a new technique for promotion of the figures of merit during determination of metal species: Improvement in the performance of pre-mixed burner

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Shiraz University, Shiraz 71454, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Shiraz University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanotechnology Research Center, Shiraz University, Shiraz, 71454, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new micro/nano bubble-modified flame atomic spectrometry has been introduced for promotion of some figures of some merit during determination of metal elements such as Pb(II) and Sn(II) via controlling the stoichiometry of an acetylene/air flame by bubbles generated through sonoelectrochemistry on the surface of nanocarbons such as carbon dots (CDs). For this purpose, the sonoelectrochemical cell is connected to a pre-mixed burner to generate both oxygen and hydrogen micro/nano bubbles during applying direct-current (DC) potential such as +8.00V (for oxygen) or −8.00V(for hydrogen) to a two-electrode system including a graphite rod and stainless steel inside a solution of 0.5M NaCl as electrolyte. According to the results, introduction of each oxygen or hydrogen micro/nano bubbles to the flame atomizer not only has strong influence on controlling the stoichiometry (oxidizing and reducing behavior) and temperature of the flame, but also prevents from flashback of the flame into the pre-mixed burner. Major improvements is also observed in some figures of merit such as linearity, sensitivity, detection limit, and the selectivity during analyses of elements such as Pb(II) or Sn(II) via modification of atomic absorption/emission spectrometry (AAS, AES) with micro/nano bubbles. Generated bubbles also play significant roles like releasing and chelating agents during analysis of Pb(II). Good agreements have been evaluated between bubble-modified AAS and inductively coupled plasma during analyses of Pb(II) in some types of rice samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Atomic spectrometry is an analytical technique that is used for qualitative and quantitative determination of perhaps ∼70 elements [1–3]. Sensitivity of atomic methods lies typically from the μgmL−1 to ngmL−1 ranges [4]. Additional virtues of these methods are speed, convenience, selectivity, and moderate cost [1,4]. Spectroscopic determination of atomic species can only be performed on a gaseous medium in which the individual atom is well separated from each other [1,5,6]. Consequently the first step in all atomic spectroscopic procedures is atomization, a process in which the sample is volatilized and decomposed in such a way to produce an atomic gas [5]. The efficiency and reproducibility of the atomization step determine the method's sensitivity, precision, and accuracy [1,5,7]. As a result, atomization is considered as the most critical step in any atomic spectroscopies [5,7].

Atomizer is effectively an analog of the sample holder (cuvette) in the UV–vis spectroscopy [8]. This module provides a vehicle for atomization during formation of atomic vapor (atom reservoir) [4,8]. In the atomic spectroscopy, various atomizers such as electrical arc/discharge, plasma, flame, and graphite furnace are used [1,4,5,9]. In the electrical arc/discharge atomizer in spite of its simplicity, it seriously suffers from low reproducibility [4]. In addition, atomizers such as arc/discharge and plasma are suitable only for atomic emission spectroscopy (AES) [5]. Whereas, atomizers like graphite furnace and flame are suggested in both atomic absorption and emission spectroscopies (AAS and AES) [2,5,9,10].

In any flame atomizer, only liquid samples can be introduced through the nebulizer via formation of droplets [5]. A sub-population (∼10%) of these droplets is then introduced into the flame [5]. In this atomizer high-pressure gas is the oxidant, with the aerosol containing oxidant being mixed subsequently with the fuel [1,4,5].

Two types of general burners in atomic spectroscopy include turbulent flow (or total consumption) and laminar flow (or pre-mixed) burners. In the former burner, total amount of independently introduced fuel and oxidant are mixed with each other to form flame [11]. This burner in spite of its safety and simplicity has serious challenges such as high fluctuation and low thermal stability [2,5]. This burner is often applicable in AES [5,12]. The most common burner type is the pre-mixed burner that has a long path length [12]. As the beam of light is passed through the broad flame, long optical path provides acceptable stability in the AAS. Formation of laminar flame is considered as another advantage of this burner, due to the pre-mixing of fuel and oxidant in a container before introduction to the flame [12].

In the pre-mixed burner as the flow rate increases, the flame rises until reaching a point above the burner [5,11]. Stable flame is provided in region at which the same values are estimated for gas flow rate and the burning velocity [2]. Consequently as soon as the gas flow rate does not exceed the burning velocity, the flame propagates itself back into the pre-mixed burner that leads to have flashback [5]. Compared to the total consumption burner, this event not only prevents the use of pure oxygen as oxidant in the pre-mixed burner but also limits the introduction of nitrous oxide to special procedure during operation with AAS [5,6,13].

From another aspect, both emission and absorption spectra are affected in a complex way by variations in the flame temperature [1]. Higher temperatures tend to promote the sensitivity via enhancement in the total atom population of the flame [1]. With certain elements such as the alkali metals, however increase in atom population is more than the offset caused by the loss of atoms during ionization, but the flame temperature determines the relative number of excited and unexcited atoms in the flame. In an air/acetylene flame, for example, the ratio of excited to unexcited magnesium atoms can be computed to be ∼10E−8 [5]. Whereas in the oxygen/acetylene flame which is ∼700°C hotter, this ratio is evaluated to be ∼10E−6 [5]. Control of the flame temperature is thus of prime importance for improving some analytical parameters such as sensitivity and selectivity during analysis of metal species with atomic spectrometric techniques [1,5].

During the last decade, micro/nano bubbles due to their significant advantages such as long lifetime, high stability and involving high pressure have possessed much attention [1,5]. It seems that introduction of bubbles into the atomizers in AAS and AES may significantly promote some figures of merit during analysis of various metal ions. To reach this purpose hereby in this study a new micro/nano bubble-modified flame AAS technique is introduced in detail.

All the reagents were from their analytical grades. Materials such as KCl, KNO3 and NaCl were from Merck; Darmstadt, Germany. Ethylenediamine tetra acetic acid (EDTA) was related to Fluka. Stock solution of 0.5M NaCl (Merck; Germany) in water was prepared via dissolving 1.462g of NaCl in 50.0mL triply-distilled water. Nanocarbons such as carbon dots (CDs), graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and their hybrids (mixture) were synthesized by exfoliation method using a sonoelectrochemical cell [14]. A pressure senor (model: 5612-015) was also utilized to record the pressure gradient during formation of micro/nano bubbles. In addition, the quantity of oxygen introduced to the burner during formation of micro/nano bubbles was quantified using an oxygen meter (model: Lutron DO-5510). A Ni–Cr thermocouple was also adopted to study the effect of bubbles on the flame temperature.

Stock solutions (1000μgmL−1) of Pb(II) and Sn(II) were generated via dissolving lead nitrate (Merck; Germany) and tin chloride (Merck; Germany) in two 50.0mL volumetric flasks. Standard solutions of at ngmL−1 levels were daily generated via dilution of the stock solutions with triply-distilled water. Also, stock solutions of (1000μgmL−1) of F− and PO4 3− were generated using NaF (Merck; Germany) and Na3PO4 (Merck; Germany) in two 50.0mL volumetric flasks to study the interfering effect during Pb analysis.

In this study, micro/nano bubbles of O2 and H2 have been synthesized using the sonoelectrochemical method [14]. The schematic of the sonoelectrochemical cell is shown in Fig. 1 . As clearly shown, the electrochemical cell consists of a Teflon cubic cell with 10cm×10cm×10cm dimensions. A two-electrode system includes a graphite rod with 0.8cm diameter and a stainless steel (Tip: 316) horn-shaped electrode with 2.0 and 3.5cm bases. This two-electrode system is then positioned next to each other at the top and bottom faces of the reaction cell. Two sonicators (model: 300E13TR-1) with ∼900kHz are also positioned next to each other in the other two sides (left and right) of the cell. The pH of the electrolyte in the cell is simultaneously detected using a pH meter (model: Lutron pH-20). Also the temperature of the electrolyte is controlled using two peltier coolers (model: 12706 peltier module TEC.) through a thermostat. The amount of current passed through the electrolyte is also fixed via adjusting the level of the electrolyte inside the cell using a level meter (model: Acvm 514 Techron). A fixed direct current (DC) potential (+8.00V or −8.00V) is applied to the two-electrode system using a potentiostat. Various parameters such as pH, sonication radiation, and applied bias are automatically operated through a program written in Visual Basic.

The synthesized micro/nano bubbles are characterized via following the responses of gas pressure sensor and the oxygen meter (model: Lutron DO-5510). For this purpose, briefly the pressure gradient as well as concentration of oxygen in parts per million (ppm) levels is recorded using T-way glass tubing, in which direct flow of gas is carried through two side arms and the sensors are individually positioned at the third side of this glass tubing. The enhancement in the concentration of each O2 or H2 during micro/nano bubble formation is evidenced via photographic imaging the burner using a charged coupled device (CCD, model: TSCO TW-1600K). Also the stability of synthesized micro/nano bubbles is evaluated via following the surface tension of the aqueous solution using photographic imaging according to the procedure reported in Ref. [15].

It should be noted that in this study, due to the formation of micro/nano bubbles on the surface of nanomaterial-based fluids such as suspension of CDs, it is impossible to characterize the morphology and size distribution of the micro/nano bubbles using electron microscopy. In addition, as a result of adoption of electrochemical method for synthesis of micro/nano bubbles [16] as well as owing to the importance of fine bubbles for supersaturation of the aqueous electrolyte, consequently it is preferred to adopt “micro/nano bubble” term for the generated bubbles using the proposed procedure.

In order to study the effect of bubbles on the performance of burner, the micro/nano bubble-generating system is situated as the bottom of the pre-mixed burner of an AAS instrumentation system (Shimadzu) to generate micro/nano bubbles of H2 and O2 on the surface of hydrophobic supports such as CDs by the sonochemical exfoliation [14]. Fig. 1, S.P. shows the SEM images of synthesized nanocarbons. To direct the electrochemically generated bubbles into the pre-mixed burner, a trace fraction (∼5%) of air as oxidant is therefore introduced to the electrolyte through a porous rock to supersaturate the electrolyte from oxygen during formation of fine bubbles inside the electrolyte solution. The synthesized micro/nano bubbles of oxygen or hydrogen are finally introduced into the cavity of the pre-mixed burner.

To generate gas micro/nano bubbles, a fixed volume (150.0mL) of 0.5M NaCl is selected as electrolyte. The pH of the solution is set using phosphate buffer solution. The temperature of this solution inside the cell is adjusted to 25±1°C. In the fabricated sonoelectrochemical cell, amperometry detection system is followed through the two-electrode system. The electrochemical exfoliation process is then carried out by applying a fixed DC bias (current: ∼200mA, voltage: ±8.00V) for generation of oxygen and hydrogen micro/nano bubbles on the surface of hydrophobic supports such as CDs.

Parameters related to the absorption and emission of Pb(II) and Sn(II) as selective detection systems are operated according to the AAS catalog (Shimadzu) using Pb and Sn hollow cathode lamps (model: Shimadzu). In this study, background correction is also evaluated using the D2 lamp, equipped in the AAS instrument.

To validate the proposed analytical approach, the results related to the AAS, micro/nano bubbles-modified AAS and inductively coupled plasma (ICP) have been compared to each other during analysis of Pb(II) in different kinds of rice by standard addition method. For this purpose, sample preparation has been achieved according to the procedure reported in the annual book of ASTM standards [17]. Based on this procedure, briefly a portion of rice grains is cleaned and ∼10.0g of the sample is dried at 105°C for ∼48h. Then, the sample is digested by ∼50.0mL of nitric/perchloric acid (Merck, 70%, V/V) [17]. The mixture is swirled and allowed to stand for half-an-hour. Afterward, the suspension is washed with ∼25.0mL of nitric acid (Merck, 0.1M) for several times and finally is dried on a mantle at temperature to ∼50°C for 0.5h until about less than 3.0mL of a transparent liquid is obtained. Subsequently, 25.0mL of the deionized water is added and mixed vigorously to complete the digestion process. The lead-containing real samples are then analyzed with using Pb hollow cathode lamp at 283.3nm wavelength with 0.2nm bandwidth.

Micro/nano bubbles are micro/nano-scaled gas cavities that are often generated at the interface of hydrophobic (water repellent, lyophobic) surfaces in liquids. The mechanism of bubbles formation is based on the nucleation of gas at the hydrophobic surface from a supersaturated solution, leading to trap the atmospheric gases [18]. However formation of bubbles is thermodynamically unfavorable, but the lifetime of bubbles is reached events to the orders of hours [18].

Micro/nano bubbles are therefore generated during formation of a bridge between hydrophobic surfaces with large aspect ratio [18]. Micro/nano bubbles grow or shrink due to the diffusion based on the over- or under-saturation of the surrounding media with the dissolved gas at different cavity pressures [19]. Therefore micro/nano bubbles provide under excess pressure as the surface tension that causes an inclination during reducing their surface area as well as their volume [20,21].

Compared to the fine bubbles, micro/nano bubbles can survive a long time [18]. This phenomenon points to the adequate buoyancy between micro/nano bubbles and solution that releases contained gas at the surface before dissolving process [22]. Consequently, micro/nano bubbles are simply generated by the strong attraction occurred between hydrophobic surfaces and solvent [18]. These intrinsic behaviors of micro/nano bubbles [20,21] make them suitable for various processes such as aeration, mass transfer, and probably controlling the stoichiometry of fuel/oxidant mixtures.

To study the influence of micro/nano bubbles in atomic spectrometry, the effect of the formation of micro/nano bubbles has been investigated on the surface of hydrophobic nanocarbons such as CDs, GQDs and their hybrids (mixtures), synthesized using the sonochemical exfoliation method during applying +8.00V to the electrode system. For this purpose, surface tension [23–26] of the electrolyte solution containing each type of CDs, GQDs and hybrids is selected as detection system (Fig. 2 ).

Fig. 3 shows the effect of solution containing carbon nanostructures on (A) surface tension, (B) rate of sample introduction to AAS as well as stability during applying C) 0.0V and D) +8.00V to the electrode system. According to the results (Fig. 2A, S.P.), maximum surface tension is evaluated for CDs, compared to that of QGDs and hybrid. This phenomenon is probably attributed to large active surface area as well as more hydrophobicity of CDs.

Compared to the general pre-mixed burner (1.15mLmin−1), the velocity of sample introduction is evaluated to be 1.50mLmin−1 for CDs, 1.28mLmin−1 for QGDs and 1.33mLmin−1 for hybrids (Fig. 2B, S.P.). Maximum quantity of sample introduction for CDs once more exhibits the effective role of CDs during formation of micro/nano bubblers. Significant stability is estimated for solution containing CDs during following surface tension at different time scales during applying +8.00V (Fig. 2C, S.P.) and 0.00V (Fig. 3D, S.P.) to the electrode-system. These results again reveal the effective role of nanomaterials specially CDs for generating bubbles with maximum stability during promotion in the gas pressure and subsequently formation of higher vacuum in the nebulizing system by the “Ventury” effect [27]. Consequently, CDs has been selected as hydrophobic nanocarbons for formation of micro/nano bubbles and their introduction to the pre-mixed burner system.

As is expected ionic strength is considered as another effective factor for generation and stabilization of micro/nano bubbles during formation of excess charge [28,29]. Therefore factors such as long-range attraction and slow diffusion cause solid–liquid interfaces to have tendency toward self-organization of micro/nano bubbles, causing to have bubbles with prolonged existence [28,30]. In this study, effects of various kinds of electrolytes such as NaCl, KNO3, KCl and NaNO3 have also been studied in detail. For this purpose, the influence of 0.5M concentration on the pressure gradient of the supporting gas (air) is evaluated during applying +8.00V to the electrode system according to the histogram shown in Fig. 3A, S.P. As clearly shown, the same behavior is evaluated during testing different electrolytes such as NaCl, KNO3, etc. Consequently, solution of NaCl was selected. To optimize the concentration, several concentrations of NaCl ranging between 0.0 and 1.0M have been tested. According to the results (Fig. 3B, S.I.) maximum pressure is observed for 0.5M NaCl.

The pH of the electrolyte solution is also considered as another important factor having strong influence on the amount of micro/nano bubbles generated by the sonoelectrochemical method. The pH of the solution, not only has major effect on the ionic strength of the solution, but also promotes the formation of bubbles O2 and H2 during the redox reaction. In this study, effect of pH has been evaluated via using universal and phosphate buffer solution ranging between 4.0 and 9.0. Fig. 4, S.P. shows the influence of pH on the formation of micro/nano bubbles via following the absorbance (Fig. 4A, S.P.) and outlet gas pressure (Fig. 4B, S.P.) on the surface of nanocarbons such as CDs, QGDs, and their hybrids. According to the results, maximum sensitivity is evaluated at pH 9.0 on the surface of nanocarbons such as CDs. Therefore, this value has been selected as optimum pH during formation of micro/nano bubbles. Negative excess charge is therefore estimated for the generated bubbles during adsorption of OH− at pH 9.0.

To promote the efficiency during the formation of micro/nano bubbles, temperature of the electrolyte solution has been optimized. Temperature from one side effects the cavitation and collapse of the generated bubbles, and from the other side controls the quantity of O2 and H2 by the redox reaction according to the Nernst equation [31]. For this purpose, the effect of formation of micro/nano bubbles on the pressure of outlet carrier gas is studied as different temperatures ranging between 10 and 60°C during applying a constant potential (+8.00V) to the electrode system. Fig. 5, S.P. shows the behavior of temperature on the pressure gradient of gas during formation and collapse of micro/nano bubbles. Based on the results; maximum sensitivity is observed at room temperature. In this designed system, to control the temperature of the electrolyte solution at 25°C (as the optimum temperature) two peltier heating/cooling systems have been adopted as shown in Fig. 1. Observation of large pressure gradient (∼1.2atm.), significantly points to the effective role of micro/nano bubbles in the safety of pre-mixed burner during prevention from any flashback.

To accurately optimize the amount of applied DC potential (bias) on the quantity of gas bubbles, the influence of various applied potentials form −8.50 to +8.50V is also studied. To get the optimum value for the applied voltage, the effect of generated micro/nano bubbles on the temperature of the air/acetylene flame is evaluated. In this study, a Ni–Cr thermocouple (Tip: K) has been utilized to evaluate the effect of bubbles on the temperature. Fig. 6, S.P. exhibits the effect of micro/nano bubbles on the flame temperature during formation of (A) O2 and (B) H2 micro/nano bubbles during applying positive and negative potentials, respectively. According to the results, maximum temperature is observed during generation of O2 at +8.00V to the electrode system, whereas minimum temperature at −8.00V is probably due to the formation of cool flame during introduction of H2 bubble to the flame.

For further investigation about the kind of gas bubbles during applying DC potential to the electrode system, relationship between O2 concentrations vs. applied voltage has also been evaluated. To reach the highest efficiency during formation of gas micro/nano bubbles, the characteristics of applied bias such as voltage and current should also be evaluated more precisely. Fig. 3 shows the effect of O2 micro/nano bubbles on (A) concentration of oxygen and (B) pressure gradient at different DC voltages applied to the electrode system. As clearly exhibited during applying +8.00V, O2 gas is electrochemically generated on the surface of graphite electrode as anode during formation of CDs. Whereas, H2 gas is formed along with applying a constant negative potential (−8.00V) to the graphite electrode as cathode.

The mentioned optimized potentials have been considered as maximum threshold values that are capable to generate micro/nano bubbles using the designed system. As shown in Fig. 3 at higher potentials, formation the bubbles is so rapid that (1) the sound related to the generation of bubbles is clearly observed; and (2) rapid transferring of the generated bubbles into the flame causes sharply throwing of the water aerosols to the flame. These events then leads to provide fluctuations and suddenly reduction in the temperature of the flame (Fig. 3), followed by turning off the flame at higher applied potentials. In addition, major difference between the slope of the diagrams of each O2 concentration (Fig. 3A) and pressure (Fig. 3B) vs. voltage is probably related to the oxidation of Cl− and formation of Cl2 gas at potentials higher than +8.00V that limits the applied voltage to maximum +8.00V.

The amount of gas micro/nano bubbles is also a function of passed current through the electrochemical cell. This current is dependent to various factors such as size and morphology of both cathode and anode electrodes, mass transfer mechanism (diffusion and convection) during the sonoelectrochemical process and the resistance of the electrolyte. In this study, due to the use of stainless horn-shaped electrode and graphite rode as well as owing to the formation of nanocarbons such as CDs in the diffusion double layer of the graphite electrode, the quantity of current is estimated to be in the mA scale that clearly shows the capability of this design for large scale generation of micro/nano bubbles.

Depending on the formation of O2 or H2 micro/nano bubbles at optimum conditions, significant change is observed in the color of the flame. This phenomenon has also been evidenced via image processing of the flame during formation of oxidizing (poor) or and reducing (rich) flame during formation of each O2 or H2 micro/nano bubbles according to the photographic images in Fig. 4 . Based on the image processing, major change is observed in the color (i.e. stoichiometry and temperature) of the flame during introduction of each O2 and H2 micro/nano bubble.

More precise control of the design is simply achieved via controlling the level of electrolyte automatically using a level meter. As the stability of flame is seriously dependent to the quantity of micro/nano bubbles, therefore for the analytical purposes maximum electrical charge should be applied to the electrode-system to have minimum fluctuation and maximum stability for the flame. In this study, according to the sequential photographic images from the flame, the most reproducible flame has been estimated at current of ∼200mA. Fig. 6, S.P. shows the image processing of the flame for introduction of H2 and O2 micro/nano bubbles to the burner at different (positive and negative) voltages. During at least five sequential image processing of the blue component of the flame, the relative standard deviation (RSD %) has been estimated to be ∼9.0%, pointing to the partial stability of flame during introduction of micro/nano bubbles. In the designed system, the level of the electrolyte solution has also been controlled in a constant level for passing a direct current as large as ∼200mA. Consequently for the fabricated design cell, the selected volume is estimated to be ∼150.0mL.

To study the effect of the sonoelectrochemically generated micro/nano bubbles on the sensitivity of the AAS during analyte determination, the absorbance of 10.0μgmL−1 of Pb(II) standard solution has been followed. For this purpose factors such as ratio of acetylene to air, height of burner and alignment of the optical pass have been initially optimized to get the highest sensitivity in the absence of any micro/nano bubble.

The effect of the micro/nano bubbles on the sensitivity has been evaluated via comparison between the calibration curves of Pb(II) standard solutions before and after ultrasonic radiation as shown in Fig. 7, S.P. As shown significant enhancement up to ∼80% is observed in the absorbance of Pb(II) during ultrasonic radiation at 570–590kHz. This phenomenon is again attributed to the effective role of sonoelectrochemistry on the generation of micro/nano bubbles for enhancement the figures of merit of elements during detection by atomic spectrometry.

Fig. 5 shows the linear dynamic range of AAS and micro/nano bubble-modified flame AAS. As shown, the linear range for AAS is estimated to 10.0–40.0μgmL−1, whereas this range is estimated to 0.5–80.0μgmL−1 when introducing O2 bubbles to the flame. Figures of merit for determination of Pb(II) in the presence and absence of micro/nano bubbles have been reported in Table 1 . In this table the detection limit has been defined as the concentration of metal species giving a steady state signal equal to the blank signal plus triple values of the standard deviation of the blank. As shown, significant improvement is observed in the detection limit, sensitivity and linearity during modification of flame with micro/nano bubbles.

Significant sensitivity of micro/nano bubble-modified AAS is also confirmed during observation of no significant change in the absorbance intensity of Pb(II), when using burner heads with different slot lengths (8.0 and 10.0cm).

According to the results evaluated during the background correction using a D2 lamp, no significant background signal has been detected during introduction of micro/nano bubbles to the flame. This phenomenon again reveals the compatibility of micro/nano bubbles with flame.

Introduction of micro/nano bubble to the flame majorly decreases the interfering effect of some foreign species. For instance, according to the Shimadzu catalog of Pb(II) [32], no cationic interferences have been reported for the air/acetylene flame during analysis of Pb(II) with AAS. Whereas a number of anionic interferences such as phosphate, carbonate, iodide, fluoride and acetate suppresses Pb(II) absorbance from at least ten-fold excess of the Pb(II) concentrations.

Histograms in Fig. 6 clearly show the effect of O2 and H2 micro/nano bubbles on the elimination of (A) 50- and (B) 100-fold excess of foreign species such as F−, CO3 2−, PO4 3− for analysis of 10.0μgmL−1 of Pb(II). As shown (Fig. 6), less interfering effect has been observed when introducing O2 micro/nano bubbles to the flame. This effect is probably attributed to the presence of repulsive the interaction between micro/nano bubbles with excess negative charge at pH 9.0, which significantly prevents introduction of anionic species to the flame. Consequently, negative excess charge of micro/nano bubbles provides the effective role of bubbles to act role like chelating agent during analysis of metal species such as Pb(II).

The masking behavior of micro/nano bubbles has also been evidenced via promotion of the cavitation bubbles during the sonolysis. Fig. 7 clearly shows the effect of ultrasound on the efficiency of O2 and H2 micro/nano bubbles on the elimination of (A) 50- and (B) 100- fold excess of F− as interfering agent during analysis of 10.0μgmL−1 of Pb(II). Effective role is therefore evaluated for the cavitation bubbles generated by the sonoelectrochemical process.

Excess negative charge of the generated micro/nano bubbles has also significant influence in playing role as chelating agent according to the results shown in Fig. 8 . Based on the results, partially the same role is evaluated for the generated micro/nano bubbles, compared to general chelating agents such as EDTA. Therefore micro/nano bubbles are considered as new types of chelating agents during metal analyses by atomic spectrometry.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of thermally-stable elements by bubble-modified flame
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the effective role of bubble-modified flame on the atomic analysis of thermally-stable elements, Sn(II) has been selected as the detection model. For this purpose, figures of merit of different standard solutions of Sn(II) ranging between 1.0 and 300μgmL−1 have been evaluated in detail. The results are shown in Table 2 .

As higher sensitivity is evaluated for Sn(II) in a reducing flame, therefore in this study, reducing flame has been generated via introduction of H2 micro/nano bubbles to an air/acetylene flame. However the sensitivity during following the absorbance of Sn(II) in the bubble-modified air/acetylene flame (calibration sensitivity: 0.3μLμg−1) is not comparable with that in the nitrous oxide/acetylene flame. However a linear range with acceptable correlation coefficient (R =0.935) is evaluated for Sn(II). The calibration curves are shown in Fig. 9 . This observation points to this fact that to some extent it is possible to analyze thermally stable elements such as Sn(II) using the bubble-modified flame even in a sensitive spectroscopic techniques such as AES.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of micro/nano bubbles in atomic emission spectroscopy
ce:section.ce:para: To study the effect of micro/nano bubble on the AES, Pb(II) was again adopted as model. For this purpose, the spectrometer was set at 405.8nm wavelength with 0.1nm bandwidth. Fig. 10 shows the calibration curves of Pb(II) in the presence and absence of micro/nano bubbles. As clearly shown, when introducing micro/nano bubbles, significant improvement is observed in the calibration sensitivity (0.390mLμg−1) and linear range (1.0–100.0μgmL−1), compared to the general AES, revealing the suitability of bubble-modified flame for both AES and AAS.

The reliability of this research has also been adopted via comparison between the results of AAS, bubble-modified AAS and inductively coupled plasma (ICP) by the standard addition method. For this purpose, different volumes of Pb(II) standard solution (5.0μgmL−1) have been added to the real sample solution based on the recommended procedure [33,34]. For this purpose, the ICP was selected as acceptable detection system whose reliability has been previously evaluated using lead standard reference material according to the ASTM No.: F963-11. According to the results (Table 3 ) compared to the ICP, lower relative error percentage (−5.33%) is evaluated that clearly reveals the reliably of the micro/nano bubble-modified flame. Consequently compatibility between the two independent analytical methods clearly reveals the accuracy of the measured data by the recommended method.

In this research, a new micro/nano bubble-modified flame atomic spectrometry has been introduced for promotion of figures of some merit during determination of metal ions via controlling the stoichiometry of an acetylene/air flame by bubbles generated through sonoelectrochemistry on the surface of CDs. Effect of bubbles has various effective role such as (1) promotion of the safety of the pre-mixed burner via prevention from flashback; (2) controlling the stoichiometry of the flame via selective generation of O2 and H2 micro/nano bubbles; and (3) promotion of some figures of merit such as sensitivity, selectivity, linearity, and detection limit in AAS and AES. Consequently, this technique seems to be suitable for simply conversion with more sensitive analytical methods such as graphite furnace – AAS, hydride generation – AAS, cold vapor-AAS, or even ICP during the atomic analysis of most elements. To the bests of our research, this study is the first report in which significant improvement is provided in the performance of a pre-mixed burner in the atomic spectrometry.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to acknowledge the support of this work by Shiraz University Research Council.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.sna.2015.03.044.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Atomic absorption, atomic fluorescence spectrometry and flame emission spectrometry
Simultaneous preconcentration of cobalt, nickel and copper in water samples by cloud point extraction method and their determination by flame atomic absorption spectrometry
Thermospray flame furnace atomic absorption spectrometry (TS-FF-AAS) – a simple method for trace element determination with microsamples in the (g/l concentration range
Determination of metals in wine with atomic spectroscopy (flame-AAS, GF-AAS and ICP-AES); a review
Atomization efficiency of total consumption atomizer-burners in flame photometry
Graphite furnace atomic absorption elemental analysis of ecstasy tablets
Direct trace elemental analysis of solids by atomic (absorption, fluorescence, and emission) spectrometry
Numerical simulations of Lewis number effects in turbulent premixed flames
Spectrochemical analysis by atomic absorption and emission
An Introduction to Analytical Atomic Spectrometry

Micro/nano bubble-modified flame atomic spectrometry as a new technique for promotion of the figures of merit during determination of metal ions: Improvement in the performance of pre-mixed burner, M.Sc. thesis

Colloid. Surf. A: Physicochem. Eng. Asp.
Accumulation of dissolved gases at hydrophobic surfaces in water and sodium chloride solutions: implications for coal flotation

Development and validation of routine analysis methods for the determination of essential, nonessential, and toxic minor and trace rlements in cereal and cereal flour samples by inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: M.M. Doroodmand terminated his under graduate studies in the pure chemistry in the chemistry department of Shiraz University, Shiraz, Iran. He then terminated his M.Sc. and Ph.D. studies in Analytical Chemistry at the Chemistry Department of Shiraz University (Iran) in 2002 and 2008, receptively with Prof. A. Safavi and Mr. N. Maleki. His Current research interest is fabrication of gas sensor, synthesis and characterization of carbon nanostructures, graphene, metal nanoparticles and also the applications of micro/nano bubbles and aeration, nebulization and chemical reactions.

M. Mehrtash terminated her under graduate studies in the pure chemistry in the Azzahra University, Tehran, Iran. She is terminating her M.Sc. in analytical chemistry with Dr. M.M. Doroodmand. Her thesis is based on the synthesis of Graphene quantum dots interest Chemistry department of Shiraz University, Shiraz, Iran.
